coredata.dc:title: High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Green synthesis and application as “on-off” fluorescent sensors for specific Fe3+ ions detection and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Lai, Chunmei
Lin, Simin
Xiong, Lei
Wu, Yuxin
Liu, Chao
Jin, Yanqiao
coredata.dc:description: A nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)-based “on-off” fluorescent nanosensor was profitably synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and o-phenylenediamine for the determination of Fe3+ and imaging towards cancerous cells in vitro. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited excellent photoluminescence and favorable biocompatibility. The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra were observed and the maximum emission wavelength was 483 nm at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The fluorescent N-CQDs had a selective and sensitive response to Fe3+ with a limit of detection of 0.177 μM and an allowable detection range of 0–110 μM. The sensor was applied for the determination of Fe3+ ions in tap water with satisfactory results. Moreover, the N-CQDs provide a practical and promising method for cellular imaging due to their excellent fluorescence potency and negligible cytotoxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fe3+ ions detection
Fluorescence probe
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: FT-IR and XPS of the N-CQDs
TEM and XRD of the N-CQDs
Fe3+ ions sensing capacity of N-CQDs
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs by Fe3+
Application of N-CQDs in tap water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) XPS survey scan, (c-e) High-resolution XPS spectra of the N-CQDs: (c) C 1 s, (d) N1s, and (e) O 1 s.
(a) TEM illustrations, (b) the relevant size distribution and (c) XRD pattern of the N-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of N-CQDs. The interposition in (a) displays the digital image of N-CQDs aqueous solution under visible (left) and ultraviolet (right) radiation (365 nm); (b) FL emission spectra of N-CQDs at different exciting waves.

(a) Comparison of the PL quenching effect of the synthesized N-CQDs with diverse ions (0.1 M). (b) FL emission spectra of N-CQDs at different Fe3+ concentrations (c) Linearly proportion between (F0 F)/F0) and Fe3+ ions strength.
Schematic diagram of the mechanisms of fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions.
Fluorescence decays of the N-CQDs in the absence or presence of Fe3+ (Ex = 375 nm, Em = 483 nm).

(a) Fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs solutions at different pH; (b) Dependence of the fluorescence emission intensity of the NaCl concentration (0–1 M); (c) The relationship between F/F0 of N-CQDs and storage times.
(a) Confocal fluorescent microscopy images of HepG2 cells after incubation with N-CQDs (50 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 4 h. (b) Toxicity of N-CQDs towards HepG2 cells using the MTT assay.
Comparing fluorescent nanoprobe analysis parameters for Fe3+ detecting.
Detection of Fe3+ ions in real tap water determined by the fluorescence probes based on the synthesized N-CQDs.

head.ce:title: High quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: Green synthesis and application as “on-off” fluorescent sensors for specific Fe3+ ions detection and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou 350108, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China National R&D Center for Tungsten Technology, Xiamen Tungsten Co., Ltd, Xiamen 361009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China National R&D Center for Tungsten Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China National R&D Center for Tungsten Technology, Xiamen Tungsten Co., Ltd, Xiamen 361009, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)-based “on-off” fluorescent nanosensor was profitably synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and o-phenylenediamine for the determination of Fe3+ and imaging towards cancerous cells in vitro. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited excellent photoluminescence and favorable biocompatibility. The excitation-dependent fluorescence emission spectra were observed and the maximum emission wavelength was 483 nm at the excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The fluorescent N-CQDs had a selective and sensitive response to Fe3+ with a limit of detection of 0.177 μM and an allowable detection range of 0–110 μM. The sensor was applied for the determination of Fe3+ ions in tap water with satisfactory results. Moreover, the N-CQDs provide a practical and promising method for cellular imaging due to their excellent fluorescence potency and negligible cytotoxicity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the richest trace metal elements in the world, Fe3+ plays an irreplaceable role in most cases of biochemical processes [1]. It is closely linked to the process of electron transfer, oxygen transport, enzyme catalysis, and cellar metabolism ascribed to its affinity with oxygen and ease of redox reaction [2,3]. Moreover, an abnormal level of Fe3+ will cause damage to the normal organism and is associated with some diseases, such as hemochromatosis, arthritis, cancer, intelligence decline, and so on [4–7]. In this regard, an accurate, sensitive, and simple method to detect Fe3+ is significant to identify and diagnose these diseases. Spectrophotometry [8], voltammetry [9], and atomic absorption spectrometry methods [10] have been employed to detect Fe3+. However, most of these techniques require specific instrumentations, which are expensive and time-consuming. Currently, the fluorescence sensors based on the quantum dots (QDs) are providing a better choice for the detection of Fe3+ due to their outstanding fluorescence characteristic, convenient monitoring, high selectivity, and rapid response. The research on fluorescence probes is mainly focused on semiconductor quantum dots, but they pose a potential threat to human health and environmental safety due to the existence of heavy metals [11,12]. In addition, the susceptible photobleaching and cytotoxicity concerns also limit their further applications [13,14]. Thus, the design of metal-free sensors with high sensitivity and environmental friendliness for Fe3+ detection is imminent.

Zero-dimensional (0D) carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a nano‑carbon material (2–10 nm) have garnered considerable attention in recent years as a result of their appreciable photoluminescence, exceptional biocompatibility, high stability, and ease of operation [15,16]. By their unique fluorescence properties and tunable photoluminescence (PL), CQDs have emerged as promising fluorescent probes and have been considered one of the most suitable candidates for the determination of Fe3+ ions [17–20].

In addition, the favorable biocompatibility and low toxicity to a variety of cell lines, as well as the fluorescence merits, greatly reinforce their potential for cell imaging [21]. So far, CQDs with different properties have been synthesized by designing different precursors, and have shown broad application prospects in various fields, especially in the field of fluorescence probes. For instance, Li et al. successfully synthesized high quantum yield (QY) N, S-CQDs, showing ion selectivity and exceptional detection sensitivity when utilized for Hg2+ detection, such fluorescent probes can also be used to detect Hg2+ ion in tap water and living cells [22]. Subsequently, Wang et al. fabricated CQDs by using some common and inexpensive biomass waste as the precursors via simple one-pot hydrothermal, and the resulting CQDs demonstrated a sensitive response towards Fe3+ (0.2–100 μM). Furthermore, a favorable recovery was acquired when the CQDs have applied the detect Fe3+ ions in pond water [1]. These research results can not only provide a new idea and basis for the synthesis of CQDs but also provide a novel alternative sensor for ion detection in practical applications. Up to now, more and more researchers are focused on using CQDs for ion detection, and, as a result, the method of using CQDs as fluorescence probes for ion detection is becoming mature gradually. However, there are also some concerns on this issue. For example, Yang et al. prepared water-soluble CQDs by using honey as the precursor via a one-step treatment under low temperature heating. The synthesis procedure was substantially environmentally friendly, however, when the resultant was functioned as fluorescence probes for Fe3+ ions detection, the limit of detection (LOD) was only 7.22 μM [23]. Similarly, Wang et al. developed B-CDs by using glucose and boric acid as the sources via hydrothermal treatment. The prepared B-CDs could quench Fe3+ effectively, and the detection range of these fluorescence probes was 0–16 μM [24]. Similarly, Yi et al. employed trans-aconitic acid and diethylenetriamine as precursors to construct a fluorescence probe (N-CDs), with a detection range of 2–50 μM [25]. Coincidentally, Ju et al. reported the preparation of nitrogen-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) via the hydrothermal method (180 °C, 12 h) by using citric acid and hydrazine as raw materials, with a QY of 23.3 % [26]. As a green, economical, and environmentally friendly biomass material, prunus avium has also been reported for the preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a QY of 13 % by hydrothermal carbonization [27]. Actually, there is still much work to be done to develop fluorescent probe materials with low detection limits and a wide detection range.

Herein, we reported an uncomplicated, green, and economical method of synthesizing N-CQDs, which was then applied as fluorescence “on-off” nanoprobes for Fe3+ detection in an aqueous solution. The obtained N-CQDs exhibited an average particle size of 3.10 nm and emitted glistening blue fluorescence under the excitation of ultraviolet light. Markable fluorescence quench of N-CQDs occurred after various concentrations of Fe3+ were added. Based on this property, N-CQDs can serve as an efficient fluorescent sensing probe to detect Fe3+ with excellent sensitivity and selectivity. The mechanism of the “on-off” process has been preliminarily discussed, which was conducive to the application in tap water. In addition, the superior biological characteristics of the N-CQDs, such as low toxicity and good biocompatibility, entrust them with great potential in the field of bioimaging.

The citric acid (CA), o-phenylenediamine, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), paraformaldehyde, and absolute ethanol (98 %) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaCl, NaOH, HCl, FeCl3, CoCl2·6H2O, SrCl2, FeSO4.7H2O, CaCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, KCl, MgCl2·6H2O, ZnSO4·7H2O, MnCl2, CuSO4·5H2O, Na2SO4, NaClO, Pb(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, AgNO3 used in this work were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 3 - (4,5 - dimethylthiazol - 2- yl) - 5 - diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), Human hepatocellular liver carcinoma cell lines (HepG2 cells), fetal bovine serum (FBS) and Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) were all purchased from Procell Life Science&Technology Co., Ltd. (Wuhan).

The citric acid (1.68 g) and o-phenylenediamine (0.9 g) were added to the mixture solution of absolute ethanol (15 mL) and ultra-pure water (15 mL) under magnetic stirring. The solution was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (50 mL) and subjected to hydrothermal treatment in an air oven at 180 ± 2 °C for 6 h until it became transparent. The prepared N-CQDs solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15 min) before dialysis (14,000 MW cutoff, Sigma). After 48 h, the target solution was lyophilized for subsequent use.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of N-CQDs were characterized by using a transmission electron microscope (Talos F200i, USA) with a field-emission gun performing at 200 kV. The Zetasizer Nano ZS90 analyzer instrument (Malvern) was performed to record the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential. Fourier transforms infrared spectrometry spectra were carried out in the spectral range of 4000 to 400 cm−1 by an FT-IR 5700 spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy was acquired by using a LAMBDA 950 spectrophotometer (USA). The X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy was operated at an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB250, Thermo Scientific) with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer with a calibrated integrating sphere system (France). The imaging of cells was obtained by employing a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, TCS SP8, Leica, Germany). The XRD patterns were derived from an Ultima III X-ray model diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) at a scanning rate of 10 o/min and with Cu Kα radiation in a reflection mode over a 2θ range from 15 to 40°. The lifetime was measured steady/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS980, England). The pH value was determined by using a pH meter (STARTER 3100, OHAUS, USA).

Different ions (Fe3+, Co2+, Sr2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Na+, SO4 2−, ClO−, Pb2+, Al3+ and Ag+) with a concentration of 0.1 M were added into the N-CQDs solution (150 μg/mL), respectively, and the fluorescence intensity under excitation at 420 nm was observed. Then, Fe3+ ions with different concentrations (0–110 μM) were mixed with 300 μL N-CQDs solution (150 μg/mL), respectively, diluted to 1.0 mL, and then adjusted to pH 2–8 with HCl or NaOH solution. Stirred the mixture gently and let it stand in the dark. Afterward, a fluorescence spectrometer was used to record their fluorescence intensity comparison.
The detection was applied to the tap water collected in our laboratory (Fuzhou, China). All these experiments in this work were measured three times and averaged.

The QY measurements of N-CQDs were performed regarding established procedures in the literature [28]. Quinine sulfate (QY was 54 % under 360 nm excitation in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution) was selected as the standard. Calculation reference formula of N-CQDs refers to Eq. (1):
(1)
ψ =
ψ r
I ×
A r
/
I r
× A
×
η /
η r
2
where ψ, I, A, and η represent QY, fluorescence emission intensity, absorption under excitation wavelength irradiation, and the refractive index of the solvent, respectively. While the subscript r stands for the quinine sulfate solution (standard substance).
HepG2 cells were civilized in DMEM (encompassing 10 % FBS) under a 5 % CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C.

The cytotoxicity of N-CQDs was determined by MTT assay. First, seeded cells at a concentration of 5 × 103 (100 μL) in a 96-well plate. After overnight incubation, replaced the previous medium with a 100 μL fresh medium containing contrasting concentrations (0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80 μg/mL) of N-CQDs for overnight culture. After that, the cells were cultured with MTT (0.5 mg/mL, 100 μL) for an additional 4 h. Next, added 100 μL of DMSO to each well for dissolving formazan crystals in a shaker (100 rpm, 10 min), and measured the absorbance at 490 nm by a TECAN Infinite F200 Microplate Reader.

To evaluate the cellular uptake of synthesized N-CQDs, a cell imaging experiment was employed. Specifically, the cells were incubated on a 12-well plate (3 × 105 per well) overnight until adherent. The medium was aspirated before washing thoroughly with PBS, followed by incubation with 50 μg/mL of N-CQDs solution in DMEM for 4 h. Afterward, cells were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde (15 min). Finally, images of the cells are taken using the CLSM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR and XPS of the N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical composition of N-CQDs was identified by FT-IR analysis (see Fig. 1a). The peculiar crests appeared on 3431 and 741 cm−1, indicating the stretching vibration peaks -OH and -NH groups, respectively, which were the hydrogen bonding within the N-CQDs [29]. The absorbing bands at 1640 cm−1 were imputed to the flexure vibration peaks of CO-NH and CO groups [30]. The bands at 1450 and 1040 cm−1 were attributable to the CN and C-O-C functional groups, respectively [31]. The absorption peak at 1270 cm−1 was generated by the in-plane bending vibrations of OH [32]. Besides that, the feature peak at 1640 cm−1 is attributed to CN expansion and contraction vibration [33], implying the successful doping of nitrogen atoms into the framework of CQDs.

The surface composed of the obtained N-CQDs was appraised by XPS. The XPS spectra demonstrated that three kinds of the element were included (Fig. 1b), and the peaks appeared at 530.87, 397.31, and 283.25 eV contrapposto the O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s, respectively [34]. Deconvolution of the C 1 s signal showed three peaks (Fig. 1c) at 282.1 eV, 284.82 eV, and 284.82 eV fitting with CC, CN, and C=O/C=N, respectively [25]. Furthermore, the N 1 s spectrogram (Fig. 1d) was disposed into a couple of sub-peaks at 400.1 eV and 400.95 eV, parallelling the C-N-C and CN groups, respectively [35,36]. Additionally, the high-resolution O 1 s spectra (Fig. 1e) exhibited two prominent peaks at 529.41 and 528.30 eV, attributing to the C-OH/C-O-C and HO-C=O groups, respectively [37]. Thus, the surface composition of N-CQDs detected by XPS was conforming to FT-IR analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM and XRD of the N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological features of N-CQDs were captured by TEM, and the computerized image (Fig. 2a) demonstrates that the N-CQDs were approximately spherical and uniformly dispersed without obvious aggregation. The corresponding particle size distribution graph (Fig. 2b) illustrated a nanometer-sized, narrow distribution with a mean value of 3.10 nm and the zeta potential measured was −1.2 mV. The hydrodynamic diameter increased marginally, mainly ascribed to the formation of hydration layers upon the N-CQD. The measured XRD pattern of the N-CQD showed an obvious peak centered at 2θ = 24.8° (Fig. 1c), which is corresponding to the (002) lattice spacing of carbon-based materials [38].

The fluorescence property was investigated by using fluorescence and UV–vis spectra. Fig. 3a showed that the N-CQDs emitted the strongest fluorescence light centered at a wavelength of 483 nm under the excitation of 420 nm ultraviolet light, indicating the synthesized N-CQDs can emit intense blue light. The Stokes shift calculated was 63 nm, which is helpful to distinguish the target from background signals in fluorescence bio-imaging. The insets of Fig. 3a showed that the N-CQDs solution was yellowish under sunlight and exhibited bright blue fluorescence visible to the naked eye under ultraviolet irradiation. The phenomenon revealed the excellent dispersion as well as the favorable fluorescence properties of N-CQDs, further proving that surface groups and particle size play a vital role in blue PL emission [39]. In the UV–vis spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. 3a), the peak centered at 274 nm was due to the π-π* hopping of the CC spline, confirming the existence of an aromatic π-system in the N-CQDs' core [40]. The hills upon 308 nm and 372 nm were attributed to the n-π* jumping of the CO bonds and NH radicals, respectively. Since the energy of the excited state was captured by the surface state, N-CQDs could yield strong FL emissions [41].

The PL nature of N-CQDs was inspected by measuring the FL emission under variance excitation. As can be seen from Fig. 3b, an excitation-dependent PL phenomenon was discerned for N-CQDs. As the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm added up to 500 nm at 10 nm intervals, the emission intensity first increased and then decreased, possibly due to the distinct sizes of N-CQDs, particle surface defects, surface states, and optical selection of N band gaps. Moreover, due to the quantum confinement effect, the energy gap decreased with the increasing size of the N-CQDs. As a consequence, the larger size of N-CQDs might be excited at the longer wavelength, and a smaller red-shift in the fluorescence maximum was found at each excitation wavelength. Accordingly, it can be inferred that the red shift of fluorescence maximum with varying excitation wavelengths was caused by different sizes of the particles in conjunction with multiemissive states [42,43]. Upon 420 nm ultraviolet light excitation, the FL intensity of the emission light reached the strongest, indicating that the synthesized N-CQDs can emit intense blue light. When the excitation light wavelength was red-shifted or blue-shifted from 420 nm, the intensity of the emitted light peak gradually decreased. This phenomenon could be ascribed to the inherent characteristic of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials. This sort of excitation-reliant fluorescence property was ascribed to the varied external defects and the different surface states of the resultant N-CQDs [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ ions sensing capacity of N-CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The regioselectivity of the nano-sensor system was performed in the presence of different ions (Fe3+, Na+, Co2+, Sr2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ag+, SO4 2− and ClO−) with a concentration of 0.1 M and at an excitation of 420 nm. Surprisingly, only the fluorescence of Fe3+ ions was quenched markedly (Fig. 4a), this shows the N-CQDs have a very high selection towards Fe3+ in aqueous. The fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs is attributed to the arrangement of surface oxygen-containing groups on their surface with Fe3+ ionized. The fluorescence intensities of the synthesized N-CQDs upon diverse concentrations of Fe3+ ions were also investigated to evaluate the LOD. Interestingly, the fluorescence strength of N-CQDs gradually lowered with Fe3+ concentration increasing from 0 to 110 μM (Fig. 4b). With the augmentation of the concentration of iron ions, the coordination between Fe3+ and surface carboxyl on N-CQDs was gradually enhanced, which could greatly enhance the non-radiative recombination of carriers and lead to the PL quenching [39]. Thanks to the heterospecific fluorogenic response of N-CQDs towards Fe3+ ionized, the N-CQDs could be widely used for Fe3+ ions assay.

In addition, following the increase in Fe3+ ions concentration, the fluorescence quench effect becomes gradually obvious, which also shows the fluorescence strength of N-CQDs is dose-dependent. The relationship between the Fe3+ ions concentration and the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0 F)/F0 was exhibited in Fig. 4c (F0 and F were the fluorescence strength of N-CQDs excited at 420 nm with and without Fe3+ ions, and the plot of (F0 F)/F0 against the Fe3+ ions density exhibited a favorable linear relation (R2 = 0.9964). In the Fe3+ concentration range of 0–110 μM, the fitted linear equation was expressed as (F
0
F)/F
0 = 0.0799c + 0.0102, where c is the concentration of Fe3+ ions. The LOD was calculated to be as low as 0.177 μM according to Eq. (2). The fascinating quenching capacity of Fe3+ could be assigned to the strong adsorption affinity in addition to the highly efficient energy transfer between N-CQDs and Fe3+ ions [39,40]. The excellent selectivity and sensitivity make the N-CQDs a promising candidate in the fields of environment and biomedicine.
(2)
LOD = 3σ / k
where σ is the standard deviation of the system blank control sample, and k is the slope of the calibration curve.

Additionally, compared with the analytical parameters previously reported (Table 1 ), this work explained more forward-looking detection results, which not only provide a sensitive method for Fe3+ detection but also open up a new idea and new basis for ion detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CQDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The strong quenching of the fluorescent intensities of N-CQDs by Fe3+ arose from many causes. One of the reasons is the half-fill-in 3d orbit of Fe3+ ions as well as their unique interaction. Another reason is the stronger interaction between Fe3+ ionic and the abundant hydroxyl radical (-OH) and/or carboxyl (-COOH) groups on the surface of N-CQDs. The large affinity enables efficient/rapid coordination/chelation, which is favorable for complex formation [17,48,49]. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching was gradually obvious with increasing Fe3+ ions strength, which was due to the functional groups of N-CQDs interacting with Fe3+ to build Fe3+-N-CQDs complexes (Fig. 5 ) [50–52]. The introduction of the electronegative nitrogen atoms played an irreplaceable role in the regulation of local electron density and promotion the coordination ability. These favorable attributes contributed to the annihilation of radiation-free electron/hole recombination and triggered the shift of excitation electrons from the surface of N-CQDs to the half-stuffed 3d orbits of the Fe3+ ions [53]. Thus, the fluorescence was quenched immediately after the introduction of Fe3+ ions into the N-CQDs solution.

To further investigate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+, fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 6 , the lifetime of original N-CQDs solution (τ1: 9.77 ns) and N-CQDs solution containing Fe3+ (τ’1: 9.83 ns) was quite close, excluding dynamic quenching. These results suggested that static quenching occurred between N-CQDs and Fe3+. In detail, the Fe3+ ion interacted with the amine groups on the surface of the N-CQDs, and then the electrons in the excited state transferred to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe3+, leading to the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs with the nonradiative electron-hole annihilation [54].

The pH value of the original N-CQDs solution was found to be 3.28 through pH meter measurement. In order to evaluate its pH dependence, the FL emission intensity of N-CQDs solution at different pH (2–8) was recorded. As displayed in Fig. 7a, the fluorescence strength had a little increase with pH increased from 2 to 4, and then a markedly decrease was observed when the pH value continued to increase to 8, this might contribute to the protonation of amino groups in N-CQDs. Moreover, the introduction of protons into the conjugated carbon structure facilitated the enhancement of fluorescence intensity. Since the nitrogen sites in the N-CQDs were saturated with protons, the fluorescence intensity changed only slightly (pH < 5). In other words, the improved fluorescence emission intensity induced by the protonation of amino groups was due to the formation of hydrogen bonds, thereby forbidding the jump of the electron [52]. Besides, the protons could regulate the electrostatic attraction in N-CQDs [54]. When the pH was adjusted to 2–4, the stronger fluorescence intensity could improve the fluorescence quenching efficiency during the detection. In this case, Fe3+ ions existed in the form of free ions, which is conducive to the accurate detection of Fe3+.

The fluorescence stability was evaluated by comparing the PL intensities of N-CQDs solutions under diversified circumstances. The fluorescent strength of the N-CQDs solution after adding different concentrations of NaCl was measured. The results indicated the fluorescence emission strength and peak characteristics of N-CQDs (Fig. 7b) had a little change under different concentrations of NaCl (0–1 M), verifying that the N-CQDs could be used in physiological saline solution. In addition, there was not significant change in the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs after storage for three months (Fig. 7c), which confirmed the favorable photostability of the synthesized N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CQDs in tap water
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of the N-CQDs as a fluorescent nanosensor to detect Fe3+ in the real sample, tap water without further treatment in our lab as a real sample. Different concentrations of Fe3+ (100, 150, and 200 μg/mL) were added to the tap water, and the recovery of Fe3+ ions was in the range of 96.7–102.3 % as depicted in Table 2 . The relative standard deviations (RSDs) were <1.3 %, which firmly confirmed that the synthesized N-CQDs had a high selectivity and sensitivity for the determination of Fe3 + in real samples.

Considering the N-CQDs' narrow size distribution, strong fluorescence emission as well as good photostability, we hereby investigated their imaging potency towards HepG2 cells. Typically, the solutions of N-CQDs (50 μg/mL) were cultured with HepG2 cells for 4 h under the condition of 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere, and the images were collected using CLSM. Fig. 8a illustrated the cells treated with N-CQDs showed stable fluorescence with the laser channel of 405 nm, indicating N-CQDs entered the cell membrane through endocytosis. The cytotoxicity was further explored (Fig. 8b). As the N-CQDs concentration increased from 0 to 80 μg/mL, the viability of HepG2 cells decreased slightly but not significantly. When the N-CQDs concentration was 5 and 80 μg/mL, the survival rate was maintained at 92 % and 80 %, respectively. The fabricated N-CQDs have good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity, which can be used as an excellent fluorescent bioimaging agent.

A simple one-pot hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and o-phenylenediamine was employed for the successfully designed of an “on-off” fluorescent nanosensor based on N-CQD for Fe3+ detection and imaging of HepG2 cells. The prepared N-CQDs showed bright down-conversion fluorescence with an optimal emission wavelength of 483 nm and QY up to 33.55 %. A key improvement of this work is the facile, inexpensive, and environmentally friendly synthesis method of N-CQDs, which exhibit remarkable water dispersibility and excellent stability in extreme pH and high ionic strength solutions. Based on the static quenching induction effect, Fe3+ selectively quenched the fluorescence of N-CQDs with high sensitivity, the LOD was as low as 0.177 μM. Moreover, the N-CQDs were also applied to detect the Fe3+ in tap water, and the recovery ranged from 96.7 % to 102.3 % with the RSDs <1.1 %. Furthermore, N-CQDs had also been used in cell imaging due to their excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and fluorescence properties. When the N-CQDs concentration was 5 and 80 μg/mL, the survival rate of HepG2 cells was maintained at 92 % and 80 %, respectively. It is believed that the synthesized N-CQDs might be used as promising candidates for both chemical analysis and biological applications.
Credit authorship contribution statement.

Chunmei Lai: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Simin Lin: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Lei Xiong: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yuxin Wu: Conceptualization, Investigation. Chao Liu: Data curation. Yanqiao Jin: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province, China (Grant No. 2021J01593).
